skill.name: protocol-reverse-engineering
skill.description: Guide network protocol reverse engineering with packet capture, traffic analysis, protocol parsing, TLS review, and custom protocol documentation.
skill.summary: Analyze captured network traffic and document proprietary protocol behavior.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution, network, filesystem, credential, weak-crypto, and obfuscation patterns. Review found no prompt injection and no confirmed malicious automation; most command findings are markdown examples, but the skill gives dual-use guidance for MITM capture, TLS key logging, fuzzing, and packet replay. Publish with a clear authorization warning and keep the risk level at medium.

medium_findings.title: Dual-use traffic interception and TLS decryption guidance
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for dual-use risk. The playbook teaches MITM capture, TLS interception, TLS key logging, and private-key based decryption. These techniques are legitimate for owned systems, but can expose credentials and private traffic without authorization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky techniques are explicit and semantically tied to protocol analysis. I found no instruction to steal traffic, so the concern is dual-use misuse rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Active fuzzing and packet replay against network targets
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for operational security risk. The examples create packets, fuzz a target socket, and replay or modify captured traffic. This can disrupt services or violate authorization if used outside a controlled environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code examples directly send packets and fuzz a named target. The surrounding context frames this as testing, but authorization limits are not stated near the examples.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive traffic and secret material handling
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for data exposure risk. The guidance searches packet data for passwords, exports HTTP objects, extracts certificate fields, and writes TLS secrets to a temporary file. These steps can reveal sensitive data from captures.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive-data handling is clear from the packet-analysis workflow. It is likely legitimate for authorized debugging, but still needs handling warnings.

low_findings.title: Markdown code fence command-execution false positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for hidden execution. Static analysis flagged many backtick locations as Ruby or shell execution, but they are markdown code fences and command examples in documentation. They do not execute by themselves inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is documentation syntax or example shell commands. No executable script, hook, or runtime launcher was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Protocol signature and hex byte false positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for obfuscation and weak-crypto blocker findings. Hex byte strings and protocol names appear in examples that explain protocol signatures, message formats, and fuzzing inputs. No evidence found of concealed payloads or evasion logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hex values are labeled protocol constants and sample fields. Their nearby text describes analysis and protocol documentation, not obfuscated execution.

low_findings.title: SSLKEYLOGFILE keylogger keyword false positive
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for keylogger behavior. The cited environment variable is the standard browser TLS secret log file used for Wireshark decryption. It is sensitive and must be protected, but it is not a keyboard logger.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line configures TLS decryption for Wireshark and references pre-master secrets. There is no evidence of keyboard event capture or persistence.

dangerous_patterns.title: MITM proxy and SSL interception examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The playbook includes transparent proxy and SSL interception commands. These are sensitive methods that should only be used on authorized traffic.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and executable by a user. The file context is educational, but the technique can capture private communications.

dangerous_patterns.title: TLS secret logging to temporary file
dangerous_patterns.description: The playbook writes TLS secrets to /tmp/keys.log for packet decryption. This can expose session secrets if the file is mishandled.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The temporary file path and TLS key log variable are directly shown. The risk depends on local file permissions and cleanup practices.

dangerous_patterns.title: Packet fuzzing and replay examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The playbook includes boofuzz and Scapy examples that send generated, replayed, or modified packets to a target. These actions can affect live services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples call network send and fuzzing workflows. The risk is credible, but the documentation does not direct use against unauthorized systems.

content.value_statement: Unknown protocols are hard to debug without a repeatable analysis workflow. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code inspect captures, infer message formats, and document protocol behavior.
actual_capabilities: Guides packet capture workflows with Wireshark, tshark, tcpdump, mitmproxy, and Burp Suite.
Explains protocol identification using ports, banners, magic bytes, headers, and stream behavior.
Provides parser patterns for binary headers, TLV fields, length-prefixed messages, and hex dumps.
Outlines TLS metadata review, JA3 fingerprinting, certificate extraction, and controlled decryption steps.
Supplies templates for custom protocol specifications, state machines, message tables, and dissectors.
Shows active testing patterns for authorized fuzzing, replay, and packet modification.
limitations: It does not bypass encryption without keys, key logs, or lawful access to endpoints.
It cannot guarantee protocol accuracy without representative captures and validation tests.
It does not replace legal approval for intercepting, replaying, or fuzzing network traffic.
Its examples require external tools that may need separate installation and privileges.

use_cases.title: Debug a Proprietary Integration
use_cases.description: Compare successful and failing sessions to infer message boundaries, fields, and error handling.

use_cases.title: Document an Internal Protocol
use_cases.description: Turn packet captures into a readable protocol specification with transport, headers, message types, and states.

use_cases.title: Plan Authorized Protocol Testing
use_cases.description: Create a scoped workflow for capture, parsing, fuzzing, replay testing, and evidence handling.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Protocol Clues
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a packet capture from an authorized lab. Help me identify likely protocols, useful filters, and the first fields to inspect.

prompt_templates.title: Infer Message Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these observed message lengths, byte patterns, and session examples. Propose a likely header layout, field meanings, and validation checks.

prompt_templates.title: Write a Protocol Specification
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a protocol specification from my notes. Include transport, handshake, message types, field tables, state transitions, and open questions.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Authorized Test Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a controlled test plan for this owned protocol service. Include capture setup, parser validation, safe fuzzing limits, replay controls, and cleanup steps.

output_examples.input: A capture has repeated TCP sessions on port 8888 with a fixed four-byte prefix and changing payload lengths.
output_examples.output: The skill would produce a triage summary, likely header fields, suggested Wireshark filters, and checks to confirm message boundaries.

output_examples.input: A team needs to document an undocumented internal device protocol from several approved lab captures.
output: Message type table with evidence notes

output_examples.input: A researcher wants to test parser robustness for an owned service after reverse engineering the handshake.
output_examples.output: The skill would outline a bounded fuzzing and replay plan with authorization scope, rate limits, monitoring, rollback steps, and data handling rules.

best_practices: Use only authorized captures and document the scope before collecting or replaying traffic.
Keep raw captures, TLS key logs, credentials, and exported objects in protected storage with cleanup steps.
Validate inferred protocol fields with multiple sessions before publishing a specification.
anti_patterns: Do not intercept, decrypt, fuzz, or replay traffic on systems you do not own or have written permission to test.
Do not treat one packet sample as a complete protocol specification.
Do not leave TLS key logs, private keys, or sensitive packet captures in shared temporary locations.

faq.question: Does this skill run packet capture tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users decide whether to run tools in their own authorized environment.

faq.question: Can it decrypt TLS traffic?
faq.answer: Only when lawful keys, key logs, or endpoint access are available. It does not provide an encryption bypass.

faq.question: Is this suitable for production networks?
faq.answer: Use it carefully. Capture, fuzzing, and replay examples should be limited to approved scopes and maintenance windows.

faq.question: What inputs improve the analysis?
faq.answer: Representative packet captures, known client actions, server responses, timestamps, protocol guesses, and observed failures improve accuracy.

faq.question: Can it create a Wireshark dissector?
faq.answer: It can guide a basic dissector structure when message fields are known. You still need to test it with real captures.

faq.question: What tools does it reference?
faq.answer: It references Wireshark, tshark, tcpdump, mitmproxy, Burp Suite, Scapy, boofuzz, and Lua dissector concepts.
